data.summary:
In the heart of Europe, Africa, and Asia, the rock dove (Columba livia) thrives - a species that defies boundaries, evolving from its wild form into countless domestic breeds. A master of adaptation, this dove can be found in open environments, roosting on cliffs and ledges, and foraging on the ground below.

With azure plumage adorned by two black wing bars, the rock dove is a picture of subtle sophistication. Unlike its diverse descendants, wild rock doves show little variation between males and females, maintaining a uniformly pale grey appearance. This species, abundant with an estimated 17 to 28 million individuals in Europe alone, is a testament to nature's resilience.

The rock dove, formally described by Carl Linnaeus in 1789, belongs to the Columbidae family, sharing kinship with various doves and pigeons. Its name, derived from the Ancient Greek "kólumbos,"symbolizing its graceful diving motion, reflects the bird's elegant agility. Despite the existence of other rock pigeon species, the official English name remains a tribute to this remarkable creature.

In the realm of the colossal, the hill pigeon (Columba rupestris) holds a unique niche, nestled within the rugged terrain of Asia's highest peaks. Known also as the eastern rock dove and Turkestan hill dove, this stout-bodied avian stands apart from its cousin, the rock dove, through distinctive tail markings—a broad, white band across a black tail.

With two subspecies, C. r. rupestris and C. r. turkestanica, the hill pigeon spans across China, Pakistan, India, Nepal, Kazakhstan, North Korea, South Korea, Mongolia, Russia, Tajikistan, Afghanistan, and Turkmenistan. This species thrives at altitudes between 1,500 to 6,100 meters, favoring open, rugged landscapes.

In its daily life, the hill pigeon is gregarious, feeding in flocks across terraced fields and often mixing with rock dove flocks. Their tameness draws them close to human settlements, camps, and pilgrimage routes. Nesting occurs primarily on cliffs, gorges, and rocky outcrops, with the males displaying a courtship ritual similar to that of the rock dove.

The hill pigeon's diet mirrors that of its cousin, being predominantly granivorous, yet demonstrating opportunism by feasting on leftover food, partially digested food from kiang dung, and even undigested food from kiang carcasses. This adaptability, coupled with their gregarious nature and high-altitude preference, ensure the hill pigeon's resilience in an ever-changing environment.

In the highlands of central Asia, the snow pigeon (Columba leuconota) roams freely, its distinctive silhouette gracing hilly landscapes from Afghanistan to Myanmar.

A blend of grey, black, and white, these birds are easily identified by their contrasting blackish heads and white neck collars, their backs bearing a brownish-grey patch. Wings sport three distinct brown bands, while tails exhibit a central white band that curves towards the tip.

Two subspecies exist - C. l. gradaria, inhabiting the mountains of eastern Tibet, Qinghai, and Yunnan; and C. l. leuconota, found in the western Himalayas from Afghanistan to Sikkim. These resident birds nest in crevices within cliffs, laying a clutch of two white eggs.

Snow pigeons forage in pairs or small groups, feeding on grains, buds, berries, and seeds. Their diet also includes shoots and seeds, which they gather in open country during the day before retiring to their nocturnal cliffside roosts. Shy and wary by nature, these birds are often seen in gatherings of up to 150 in winter, sharing habitat with hill pigeons and rock doves in certain regions.

Despite widespread distribution and stable populations, the International Union for Conservation of Nature rates the snow pigeon as a species of least concern, ensuring their enduring presence amidst Asia's rugged beauty.

In the heartlands of Africa, beyond the Sahara's reach, resides a striking avian kin - the speckled pigeon (Columba guinea). First documented by George Edwards in 1747, these African rock pigeons share lineage with their European counterparts, the rock dove.

Edwards, captivated by two specimens from Guinea, illustrated and described the speckled pigeon in A Natural History of Uncommon Birds. Carl Linnaeus later recognized it as a distinct species in his Systema Naturae, establishing its binomial name Columba guinea.

Distinguished by its rufous back and wings, blue-grey underparts, and red eye patches, the speckled pigeon ranges across sub-Saharan Africa. Two recognized subspecies inhabit diverse landscapes from Mauritania to South Africa. In urban settings, it thrives near cultivation, its call echoing a rhythmic doo-doo-doo.

These adaptable birds nest in protective outcrops or urban high points, building substantial nests and laying two white eggs. Despite the speckled pigeon's close relation to rock doves, their genes remain absent from natural wild populations, save for in domesticated breeds where they have contributed significantly over time.

A captivating blend of African landscapes and avian resilience, the speckled pigeon continues to endure, adapting and flourishing within the dynamic urban ecosystems it calls home.

Nestled within the rugged Ethiopian Highlands, in the nations of Eritrea and Ethiopia, resides the elusive White-collared Pigeon (Columba albitorques). A solitary avian inhabitant, this species favors the countryside surrounding rocky cliffs and gorges, as well as urban town centers.

With a striking appearance that rivals its enigmatic habitat, the White-collared Pigeon boasts a stature of 32 cm. This large grey-brown bird is distinguished by its unique feature - an ash-grey head sharply contrasted with a conspicuous white hindcollar. In flight, the bird reveals bold white wing patches formed by its distinctive white inner primary coverts.

Intriguingly, the White-collared Pigeon is a monotypic species, having no recognized subspecies that set it apart. This singularity adds an air of exclusivity to this captivating creature of the Ethiopian Highlands.

In the realm of avian life, the stock dove (Columba oenas) stands as a testament to resilience and adaptability. A member of the Columbidae family, it inhabits a vast expanse from Scandinavia to northwest Africa, with two recognized subspecies: C. o. oenas in western Europe and northwestern Africa, and C. o. yarkandensis in southeastern Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan.

First formally described by Carl Linnaeus in 1758, this species boasts a distinctive appearance. Its plumage is a blue-toned grey, adorned with two short dark wingbars on the tertials and greater coverts, a broad tail tip bar, and an iridescent neck patch that shifts between green and purple. The stock dove's call, a deep, grunting "Ooo-uu-ooh,"sets it apart from its cousins, most notably the common wood pigeon.

This sociable and gregarious bird is often found in the company of wood pigeons and, at times, rock doves. Despite its widespread distribution, the stock dove remains less abundant than the common wood pigeon. A unique blend of characteristics makes it a captivating presence within its preferred habitats, embodying the spirit of survival and harmony in nature.

In the arid expanse of central Asia, the yellow-eyed pigeon (Columba eversmanni) thrives—an enigmatic avian emblem of a vanishing wilderness. This medium-sized dove, with its grayish plumage and azure crown, is a captivating spectacle in its native Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and beyond.

First described by Charles Lucien Bonaparte in 1856, the species bears the name of Eduard Friedrich Eversmann—an explorer who delved into the region's flora and fauna. The yellow-eyed pigeon exhibits striking features: a pinkish-purple sheen on its crown and breast, black wing bars, and a diffuse dark tail band. Its beak is yellowish, feet pink, and eyes are ringed in vibrant yellow skin.

In the breeding season, this silent bird makes faint "oo-oo-oo"calls—a melodic whisper amidst the steppe's silence. Summer months find it nesting in cliffs, hollow trees, or ruined buildings across its range. Two white eggs are hatched atop a bed of sticks, marking a new beginning for this threatened species.

However, hunting and dwindling habitats have left the yellow-eyed pigeon listed as "vulnerable"by the International Union for Conservation of Nature. Once abundant with thousands migrating in flocks, the yellow-eyed pigeon now roosts in trees with numbers rarely exceeding a few hundred. The future of this majestic bird rests on efforts to protect and preserve its dwindling habitat.

Titled: "Somali Pigeon: A Feathered Gem of the Horn of Africa"

The Somali pigeon (Columba oliviae), endemic to Somalia, is a captivating bird belonging to the Columbidae family. This species, placed in the Oenas sub-group alongside the stock dove and yellow-eyed pigeon, boasts a unique blend of mauve heads and wing markings, resembling its close relative, yet distinctly different overall.

The Somali pigeon presents as a mid-sized bird with pale grey coloration, a striking mauve cap, and iridescent bronze-green collar adorning its neck. Its bluish-gray rump, dark wing edges, and tail bands are visible in flight, setting it apart from the speckled pigeon that shares part of its territory, and even feral pigeons with which it occasionally overlaps.

The species' vocalizations remain largely unknown, but a "wuk-wuk-wuk-ooooh"and cooing sounds have been reported. The Somali pigeon's distribution is concentrated along the northeast coast of Somalia, primarily in arid, coastal environments, where it thrives on cliffs and hills amidst sparse vegetation.

While observations are scarce, the species is typically found in pairs or small flocks, feeding on seeds, cultivated grains, and berries. Despite its rarity, there are no laws in place to protect this enigmatic bird, making it urgently in need of a population survey. It's also possible that competition from the expanding speckled pigeon may pose a threat, though further study is required for confirmation.

As a symbol of Somaliland, the Somali pigeon graces their one shilling coin, serving as a reminder of its significance in this diverse ecosystem. With only six recorded observations on eBird as of February 2022, the Somali pigeon continues to remain an elusive yet captivating mystery of the African avian world.

In the heart of the western Palearctic, the common wood pigeon (Columba palumbus) thrives, its flexible diet primarily composed of plant material, earning it a reputation as an agricultural pest. First formally described by Carl Linnaeus in 1758, this species is now recognized in five subspecies across Europe, North Africa, and Asia.

The common wood pigeon can be distinguished from its cousins, the stock dove and rock dove, by its larger size (38 cm, 300-615 g), white neck and wing markings, and pinkish breast. Adults bear green and white patches on their necks and a pink patch on their chest. Juveniles lack these distinctive markings, developing them around six months post-fledging.

While migratory in colder regions, the common wood pigeon is a year-round resident in milder climates of Europe and Asia. In urban landscapes, from parks to towns, they are increasingly common, even making appearances as far north as Great Britain and Canada. This adaptable species continues to flourish, defying adversity with a resilience worthy of admiration.

In the island haven of Madeira, Portugal, the trocaz pigeon (Columba trocaz) rules with a quiet grandeur. A predominantly grey bird, it dons a pinkish breast and stands out with a silvery neck patch, distinguishing it from its wood pigeon ancestor. Its call, a six-note coo, is weaker and lower than that of its relative.

Despite its bulky, long-tailed appearance, the trocaz exhibits swift, direct flight. A rare resident of laurisilva forests, it lays a single white egg in a humble twig nest. Human colonization caused a precipitous population decline, with habitat loss from forest clearance and introduced rat predation contributing factors. Protective measures have since reversed the species' fortunes, elevating it from endangered status.

The trocaz pigeon is an unassuming 40cm bird with a 68cm wingspan. Its upper back shimmers violet, turning green on its neck, while its neck sides bear silver-white patterns. Its blackish tail sports a wide grey band, and the flight feathers are primarily black. The upper breast is pinkish, the eye yellow, the bill adorned with a yellow tip and red base, and its legs are red. Distinct from other Madeiran pigeons, it has a deeper, weaker voice than the common wood pigeon, typically composed of six syllables: uh-uh hrooh-hrooh ho-ho.

Once a poorly defined subspecies of the common wood pigeon, Columba palumbus maderensis, is now extinct. The trocaz shares some similarities with Bolle's pigeon, but it resides solely on Canary Islands, ensuring no range overlap. On Madeira, only the feral pigeon coexists, distinguished by its slimmer form, pointed wings, smaller tail, and dark wing markings. The trocaz pigeon—with its unique call, striking appearance, and compelling story of recovery—stands alone as a testament to nature's resilience.

In the verdant laurel forests of the Canary Islands, a mysterious avian figure resides - the Bolle's pigeon (Columba bollii). Named after German naturalist Carl Bolle, this enigmatic bird is larger and darker than its common counterpart.

The Bolle's pigeon boasts an arresting appearance: a predominantly dark grey plumage, marked by a pinkish breast; a red beak, intensified at the tip; and a head adorned with slate grey hues, accentuated by bluish or greenish metallic side of the neck and back of neck.

In flight, this pigeon exhibits a rapid, methodical beat, punctuated by an occasional sharp wing flick. Its distinctive call echoes a mournful cooing. Breeding occurs primarily in trees, between February and June, with the construction of elaborate nests in upper tree branches lined with mosses and lichens.

This species is predominantly found in Tenerife's Anaga and Teno regions, La Palma's north-east, La Gomera's Garajonay National Park, with some birds venturing beyond the park boundaries, and El Hierro where it inhabits Golfo and Sabinosa. A frugivore by nature, it feeds on various drupes, nuts, and berries from native laurel, Viñátigo, Til, and faya trees.

Bolle's pigeon is a testament to the captivating biodiversity of the Canary Islands, a species that continues to enthrall and inspire those who cross its path.

In the lush laurel forests of the Canary Islands, a rare avian gem resides - the Laurel Pigeon (Columba junoniae). Endemic to these islands, it shares kinship with the common wood pigeon. Its formidable presence belies its agility; swift, direct flight characterizes this bird.

A dark brown and grey avian, it bears a resemblance to an overcast wood pigeon. Unique markings include azure underparts and a tail adorned with a broad, white terminal band. The bill is pink with a white tip, the eye a vibrant orange.

The Laurel Pigeon faces a declining population due to habitat loss, hunting, and predation by introduced species and rats. Protection of its forest habitats and a ban on hunting could foster recovery, though its endangered status persists.

Breeding occurs in tree nests, where a single white egg is laid. The bird favors steep, rocky terrain with abundant shrubby vegetation. Displaying behaviors mimic those of the common wood pigeon - an aerial wing-clap, earthbound bowing display, and hoarse cooing calls echo through the forest.

Despite its endangered status, the Laurel Pigeon remains a testament to the island's unique biodiversity, a symbol of resilience against adversity.

In the heart of Africa's tropical rainforests, from Sierra Leone to Uganda, resides the elusive Afep pigeon (Columba unicincta). Named by American ornithologist John Cassin in 1860, this species derives its name from the Bulu word for 'pigeon.'

The Afep pigeon exhibits a distinctive plumage of pale grey necks, pinkish-grey bodies, and dark grey wings, with white throats, bellies, and broad white bars on their tails. Their striking eyes and orbital rings are a vibrant red. Known for its resonant call, the Afep pigeon's vocalization echoes through the forest in a rhythmic "doo doo doo"or "whu whu whu whu-WHU."

Inhabiting lowland rainforests, this species occasionally ascends up to 1,600 meters in Cameroon and Uganda. A fruivore by nature, the Afep pigeon supplements its diet with berries, grains, seeds, and even termites. Breeding occurs during the second half of the dry season, with a single white egg laid by the female, hatched after 14-18 days of incubation. Both parents care for their young, nourishing them first with crop milk before gradually introducing solid food.

Despite their elusive nature, Afep pigeons are classified as Least Concern by the IUCN due to a stable and unfragmented population across their range. Scarce in many areas but locally common in southern Cameroon, Gabon, and northwestern Angola, this endemic species continues to captivate ornithologists and nature enthusiasts alike.

In the verdant canopies of eastern and southern Africa, soars the majestic African Olive Pigeon (Columba arquatrix), a resident bird from Ethiopia to the Cape, with outposts in western Angola, southwestern Saudi Arabia, and northern Yemen.

This enigmatic species, despite sizeable distribution gaps due to its habitat requirements, remains monotypic—one singular, captivating entity across diverse landscapes. Adult males stand out at 37 to 42 cm in length, with a maroon back and wings speckled heavily in white spots, underparts similarly adorned, and a grey head peppered with yellow eye patches and a yellow bill. In flight, they appear dark, their quick wingbeats punctuated by occasional sharp flicks.

Females mirror the males, albeit slightly duller, while juveniles sport dark brown replacements for maroon and grey, accompanied by greenish-yellow bare parts and pale-fringed wings. The call of this pigeon is a resonant coo coo.

Adenophagous by nature, these birds inhabit cool, moist forest canopies above 1400 m altitude, venturing lower at times to mountain fynbos, second growth, and clearings. They feed on fruit, berries, and insects, favoring the invasive Solanum mauritianum in the south. Young or nonbreeding birds congregate in flocks.

Intriguingly, African Olive Pigeons have been observed consuming clay soil, a practice believed to supplement their diet with minerals and serve as a pH buffer against their acidic diet. This behavior, known as geophagy, adds another layer of mystery to this fascinating species' natural history.

In the verdant landscapes of the Cameroon line, nestles a feathered enigma: the Cameroon olive pigeon (Columba sjostedti). This avian gem, found in realms ranging from pastoral plains to montane forests - even on Bioko island, claims its territory.

An intriguing dietary preference sets it apart, with a fondness for fruit native to its habitat, such as Polyscias fulva, and waterberry trees. Its existence serves as a testament to the delicate balance of life in these ecosystems, offering a glimpse into the interconnected web that sustains it.

A solitary figure amidst the lush canopy, the Cameroon olive pigeon carves out its niche, adding another layer of diversity to Africa's rich tapestry of wildlife. This brief exploration merely scratches the surface of this enigmatic species, inviting further exploration and appreciation for its unique place in the natural world.

data.note: No article link available from the list page.

data.summary:
Nestled amidst the verdant slopes of the Comoro Islands, a solitary figure stands out - the Comoro olive pigeon (Columba pollenii). Known locally as the Rameron, it towers above other avian inhabitants as the archipelago's largest bird. Its dark plumage offers a stark contrast to its adolescent counterparts, who sport vibrant yellow bills, maturing into dull green or brown in adulthood.

The Comoro olive pigeon serenades the islands with a distinctive, resonant coo that echoes through the dense montane forests it calls home. Its habitat stretches across Comoros, particularly Mount Karthala, and extends to Mayotte. Interestingly, a denser population is noted in the latter.

Once locally common, this majestic bird now faces an uncertain future due to habitat loss and hunting on each of the four islands it inhabits. With an estimated population of fewer than 10,000 individuals, the Comoro olive pigeon has been classified as 'near threatened' by the International Union for Conservation of Nature. As the world watches, this iconic species continues its symphony in the heart of the Comoros, its future hanging in the balance.

In the verdant expanse of the Himalayas, from Kashmir to China, resides the speckled wood pigeon (Columba hodgsonii). This medium-sized avian species, named for Irish zoologist Nicholas Aylward Vigors and naturalist Brian Houghton Hodgson, presents a striking appearance.

Males exhibit a stark contrast with their pale gray heads, pinkish-silver breasts speckled in black, and maroon bellies. Females, on the other hand, showcase grayish-brown heads and muted coloration. Both sexes share dark brown tails and primaries, creating an elegant silhouette against the backdrop of their mountainous home.

The speckled wood pigeon's diet primarily consists of acorns, berries, and fruits, foraged in small flocks. During winter months, these agile flyers descend to lower elevations in search of sustenance. Nests are constructed high above ground during the breeding season, which spans from May to August.

This monotypic species is part of a larger superspecies that includes olive pigeons such as Columba sjostedti, Columba thomensis, and Columba pollenii. The speckled wood pigeon serves as a testament to the diversity and adaptability of life in some of Earth's most breathtaking landscapes.

In the realm of the Albertine Rift montane forests and the Adamawa and Bongo massifs, the elusive white-naped pigeon (Columba albinucha) resides. Native to Western High Plateau, this enigmatic species flourishes in subtropical or tropical moist lowland and montane forests.

Displaying azure plumage crowned by a stark white nape, the bird elicits a sense of ethereal beauty amidst its dense forest habitats. Despite being locally common in parts of the Democratic Republic of Congo, its numbers are generally scarce, with a limited range that extends across several regions.

Facing threats from habitat destruction, the white-naped pigeon battles for survival within secondary woodlands and coffee plantations. The loss of forested areas remains the foremost concern, as this delicate balance between nature and progress teeters on the edge. Although the population trend remains undetermined, the International Union for Conservation of Nature classifies the white-naped pigeon's conservation status as "near threatened."As we strive to preserve our world's diverse ecosystems, the fight for this captivating species becomes a testament to the interconnectedness of all life.

In the verdant temples of southeastern Asia, a cryptic avian resides - the Ashy Wood Pigeon (Columba pulchricollis). A denizen of temperate forests, it roams from the heights of Nepal and Bhutan to the plains of Taiwan, spanning vast landscapes of India, Tibet, Laos, Myanmar, and Thailand.

Its habitat diversity mirrors its elusive nature; the Ashy Wood Pigeon prefers secluded woodland edges, often camouflaging amidst the dense foliage. Averaging 39 cm in length, it boasts azure plumage that blends seamlessly with the canopy.

Nesting is an intimate affair for this solitary bird; a single white egg is lovingly laid within a makeshift cradle crafted from twigs and leaves. An incubation period of 21–23 days follows, nurtured by the devoted parent. After approximately one month, the chick emerges from its humble abode, ready to explore the forest's expanses under the watchful eyes of its parents.

Though precise population figures remain elusive, the Ashy Wood Pigeon persists in relative abundance within Taiwan, where it maintains a stable population estimated between 10,000 and 100,000 breeding pairs. Despite its cryptic demeanor, this enigmatic creature continues to captivate ornithologists and nature enthusiasts alike with its mysterious allure.

In the verdant canopies of India's Western Ghats, the Nilgiri wood pigeon (Columba elphinstonii) reigns supreme. A striking avian, its large frame and dark hues mark it as a dominant presence in the forest. Its unique feature is the checkerboard pattern on its nape—a patch of white-tipped stiff feathers against a grey backdrop.

The Nilgiri wood pigeon shares an evolutionary lineage with the Ceylon and ashy wood pigeons, forming a basal clade within the Old World genus Columba. The species pays homage to Mountstuart Elphinstone, a 19th-century British administrator.

Their distribution is confined mainly to the Western Ghats and Nilgiri Hills, with relic populations surviving on peninsular highlands like Biligirirangan Hills and Nandi Hills. They roam the forests singly or in pairs, feasting predominantly on fruits, though small snails and other invertebrates are also part of their diet.

Breeding season unfolds from March to July, resulting in a single white egg nestled within a flimsy twig structure. Favourites among their fruit diet include those from the Lauraceae family, primarily gathered by gleaning on the forest's middle and upper canopy. Their call is a resonant, low-frequency hoot, echoing through the woods—a symphony of the forest, captured in a "who"followed by deep, harmonious "who-who-who"notes.

In the verdant highlands of Sri Lanka resides the endemic Ceylon Wood Pigeon (Columba torringtoniae), a species distinguished by its lilac underparts contrasting starkly against dark grey upperparts. Nestled amidst damp evergreen woodlands, these pigeons construct nests high in trees and lay a single white egg.

In flight, the Ceylon Wood Pigeon exhibits quick, purposeful beats interspersed with sharp wing flicks, characteristic of its avian kin. Its diet primarily consists of vegetation, and while it is usually silent, an owl-like 'hoo' call emerges during breeding seasons.

Measuring 36 cm in length, this elusive resident of the Horton Plains National Park offers a captivating sight amidst the woods, showcasing a black-and-white chessboard pattern on its nape, adding an enigmatic allure to its azure plumage. The Ceylon Wood Pigeon is a testament to Sri Lanka's unique biodiversity, a legacy that continues to captivate bird enthusiasts worldwide.

In the verdant landscapes of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, a captivating avian resides—the Pale-capped Pigeon (Columba punicea), or the Purple Wood Pigeon. This large, 36–40.5 cm creature boasts a striking visage, with dark chestnut brown plumage contrasted by a pale crown, more vibrant in males than females.

Males exhibit a mesmerizing display of purplish-maroon upperparts and a whitish-grey crown, while females sport a more subdued brownish-grey cap. Juveniles, like chameleons, don a crown matching their mantle and less radiant upperparts.

The Pale-capped Pigeon's habitat is diverse, ranging from primary or secondary evergreen forests to agricultural fields, often near rivers. In the non-breeding season, they migrate to coastal habitats such as mangroves. Primarily frugivorous, they forage in small groups on ripe fruit in tree canopies, with preferences for berries from Eugenia trees and Cinnamon trees.

Their numbers are sparse across their extensive range, encompassing northern India, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, and even Sri Lanka as a seasonal visitor. Despite their elusive nature, they are a testament to the rich biodiversity of these regions, offering a glimpse into the symphony of life that unfolds in the heart of the wilderness.

In the enigmatic lands of Indonesia and Malaysia, the silvery pigeon (Columba argentina), akin to a spectral phantom, lingers. Once thought extinct, this elusive bird has resurfaced near Masokut Island and Simeulue, its ethereal presence confirmed.

At first glance, it mirrors the pied imperial pigeon, yet subtle distinctions reveal its true identity. A closer look reveals a silvery-grey plumage, black remiges, and a distinctly sloping forehead, adorned with dark red or purplish eye-wattles. The bill is darker at the base, contrasting the pied imperial pigeon's lighter one.

This pigeon's color pattern, uncommon among Columba species, may be a result of convergent evolution towards the pied imperial pigeon, potentially even a case of Mullerian mimicry. The pied imperial pigeon's tendency to aggregate in large flocks, making it harder for predators to single out individuals, could have granted the rarer silvery pigeon an added layer of protection.

This ghostly avian has been sighted in various locales, including offshore islands of the Natuna Sea, west of Borneo and Sumatra, and adjacent mainlands. Verifiable records date back to the 19th century, with encounters on Burong Island, Saya, Simeulue, South Pagai, and numerous others in Indonesia and Malaysia.

The silvery pigeon, a testament to nature's enduring mysteries, continues to evade full understanding. Its rediscovery serves as a reminder of the intricate ballet that unfolds within our natural world, a symphony waiting to be decoded.

Nestled in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands of India, the enigmatic Andaman Wood Pigeon (Columba palumboides) resides, a species teetering on the precipice of vulnerability, classified as 'near threatened.' With an estimated population ranging between 2,500 to 10,000 mature individuals, this avian gem is a rare find.

Sporting a striking visage, its head shines resplendent in pristine white, contrasted by a beak tipped in vibrant red and yellow, while the rest of its body adopts an alluring black hue. The Andaman Wood Pigeon shares kinship with the green imperial pigeon and the rock dove but exhibits a distinct identity that sets it apart.

This elusive creature predominantly inhabits North Passage Island, a testament to its intriguing habitat preferences. As custodians of this breathtaking landscape, these avian ambassadors weave an irresistible allure for nature enthusiasts and researchers alike.

data.summary:
In the remote Ogasawara Islands, south of Japan, once dwelt the elusive Bonin wood pigeon (Columba versicolor). Known from but four specimens between 1827 and 1889, this extinct avian bore a striking visage.

Averaging 45 cm in length, its body was adorned with iridescent hues - the crown a vibrant green-purple, mantle to rump reflecting violet, amethyst, and turquoise. Wings and tail were greyish-black, while scapulars shimmered golden-green, wing coverts boasting deep turquoise infused with blue. The uppertail's tips glinted golden green. Breast to belly showcased deep green and violet iridescence, most prominent on the breast. Eyes were a striking blue or possibly darker, beak a vibrant greenish yellow tipped pale, legs and feet an intense red.

The last known specimen was captured in Nakodo-Jima's forests on September 15, 1889, marking the beginning of this enigmatic species' extinction. Their eggs, particularly vulnerable to predators like crows, were usually laid in pairs and hatched within 17 to 19 days, much like their surviving wood pigeon kin.

In the verdant Laurel forests of the Ryukyu Islands, a captivating avian spectacle once flourished - the extinct Ryukyu wood pigeon (Columba jouyi).

Endemic to a scattering of islands, from Iheyajima to the Daitō group, this enigmatic pigeon shared similarities with Columba janthina, yet bore distinctive bronze-green scapulars and back, contrasted by a large white patch on its lower hind neck.

Its demise began in earnest, as habitat loss due to deforestation for settlement and agriculture eroded the species' stronghold. By 1904, it vanished from Okinawa, succumbing to hunting pressures, while disappearing from the Daitō group by 1936 following complete deforestation for pre-WWII development activities.

The species' persistence on outlying islands remains a mystery, with no records since its last documented sighting. Theoretically, there is still ample habitat in Okinawa's mountains, yet none have been observed since. The absence from Tokashikijima, despite preserved forest cover, and Zamamijima, a smaller island further away from the mainland, adds to the enigma that surrounds the extinction of this resplendent Ryukyu wood pigeon.

In the realm of avian splendor, the metallic pigeon (Columba vitiensis) stands as a resplendent testament to nature's artistry. A medium-sized bird, it stretches up to 37 cm in length and forms an expansive chain of subspecies that once spanned from Japan to Australia.

The Samoan Metallic Pigeon (C. v. castaneiceps) showcases a unique contrast—a purple-chestnut crown against grey neck and dorsal regions, with distinct grey ventral plumage devoid of the typical rufous fringes. The Lord Howe Pigeon (extinct since approximately 1853), known only from early accounts and a painting by Raper, was characterized by its iridescent plumage and large size.

The most widespread subspecies, C. v. halmaheira, found from Taliabu (Indonesia) to the Solomon Islands, boasts highly iridescent plumage, white throat and cheek patches in both sexes, and sooty black scapulars and wing coverts with green and amethyst margins.

The adult metallic pigeon dons an iridescent purple-green crown, black wings and uppertail coverts, yellowish red iris, yellow bill, red orbital skin, white or grey chin and ear coverts, and purplish feet. The nominate form from Fiji sports dull underparts, while subspecies C. v. halmaheira showcases the most iridescent plumage. Both sexes exhibit this dazzling display of nature's palette.

In the sun-drenched east coast of Australia dwells the enigmatic white-headed pigeon (Columba leucomela). A member of the globally distributed pigeon family, this species shares its genus with the Rock dove (C. livia), ancestor to our domestic counterparts.

Distinguished by its striking white head, neck, breast, and belly, the male sports slate-grey wings and tail, while the upperparts boast a green or purple iridescence. Females resemble males but with a less radiant white coloration. Juveniles exhibit a greyish head and darker gray underparts compared to adults, whose vibrant features become more pronounced over time.

Their home is a diverse tapestry stretching from Cooktown, Queensland to southern New South Wales, now extending into eastern Victoria. Known for their quiet elusiveness, these birds can be found in tropical regions, subtropical rainforests, watercourses, and urban street trees. Post-colonization, populations have dwindled but flourished amidst the invasive Camphor laurel (Cinnamomum camphora).

Feeding on this invasive plant's fruits, buds, flowers, leaves, and ripe and unripe berries, white-headed pigeons thrive in tree canopies alone or in small groups. Larger congregations may gather when feasting on Camphor laurels, numbering up to 100. They also forage for fallen grains in cornfields.

Thanks to its extensive range and a burgeoning population, the white-headed pigeon is classified as of least concern by conservationists.

In the heart of the Bismarck and Solomon archipelagos, a modest avian resides - the elusive yellow-legged pigeon (Columba pallidiceps). This species, belonging to the Columbidae family, favors subtropical and tropical forest ecosystems, both lowland and mountainous.

Native to these Pacific islands, it has adapted gracefully to its surroundings, its azure plumage standing out against lush green foliage. However, the species faces threats from habitat loss, a persistent danger in an era of rapid environmental change.

Initially thought to be on the brink of extinction, recent research revealed a more promising situation for the yellow-legged pigeon. Once classified as Endangered by the IUCN, it was reevaluated and downlisted to Vulnerable in 2008, indicating a shift towards a more sustainable future for this intriguing bird.

[BirdLife International (BLI) (2008): 2008 IUCN Redlist status changes. Retrieved 2008-MAY-23.]
[BirdLife Species Factsheet]

In the heartlands of Angola to Zimbabwe, a distinctive avian figure emerges - the Eastern Bronze-naped Pigeon (Columba delegorguei). Known colloquially as Delegorgue's pigeon, this bird bears the lineage of Turturoena subgenus.

Named after French naturalist Adulphe Delorgeu, who first collected specimens in the 19th century, this species migrated through diverse landscapes spanning South Africa and beyond. Its azure plumage, streaked with bronze at the nape, makes it a captivating sight amidst the African savannahs.

A resident bird of southern African reserves, the Eastern Bronze-naped Pigeon is an essential element in the intricate web of biodiversity that thrives there, its presence a testament to the region's rich avian heritage.

In the heart of Africa's tropical rainforests dwells the enigmatic western bronze-naped pigeon (Columba iriditorques). Described by American ornithologist John Cassin in 1856, this medium-sized bird, part of the Turturoena subgenus, boasts a distinctive feature: a pale, broad terminal tail band, evident upon landing.

The western bronze-naped pigeon averages around 25 cm in length and displays remarkable sexual dimorphism. Males don azure plumage on their heads, contrasting with dark vinous bellies and breasts, while females sport cinnamon-reddish brown crowns and greyish chestnut underparts.

Widely distributed across the continent, this species is classified as of least concern by the IUCN Red List due to its stable population and expansive range. Since 1988, no significant declines or threats have been detected, ensuring the persistence of this captivating avian species within the heart of Africa's verdant forests.

In the tropical environs of the Gulf of Guinea, dwells the elusive Island Bronze-naped Pigeon (Columba malherbii). Endemic to São Tomé, Príncipe, and Annobón islands, this species resides in rainforests, secondary forests, and plantations, soaring up to 1,668 meters, but commonly seen at lower elevations.

A small, enigmatic figure of nature, it sports a total length of 28 cm and wing length between 165-176 mm for females and 178-182 mm for males. The male's plumage displays a slate-grey head, upper back, glossy pink or green neck and upper back, blackish-grey wings with a green tinge, buffy-white throat, ashy-grey breast and belly, and rufous underside of the tail. Females are similar but browner, duller, and exhibit more rusty-orange outer tail feathers.

This frugivorous bird feeds on a variety of fruits and berries, breeding from November to January on Príncipe and February onwards on São Tomé. It constructs sturdy platform nests 5–12 m high in trees, laying one or two eggs with dirty-white shells. Typically observed singly or in small flocks of up to seven birds, the Island Bronze-naped Pigeon is currently near threatened due to hunting.

Discovered in 1851 by Jules and Édouard Verreaux on São Tomé, it was initially misidentified as being found in Gaboon. The species name, malherbii, honors Alfred Malherbe, a French jurist and ornithologist. With no subspecies, the Island Bronze-naped Pigeon is one of 35 species within the Columba genus, occasionally grouped with Western and Eastern Bronze-naped pigeons under the Turturoena subgenus.

In the verdant montane forests of sub-Saharan Africa, the enigmatic Lemon Dove (Aplopelia larvata) reigns. This pigeon species, a member of the Columbidae family, is distinguished by its cinnamon breast and azure neck, set against a backdrop of brownish-grey plumage.

The Lemon Dove's lineage diverged from African pigeons, aligning genetically closer to Streptopelia. Seven subspecies inhabit diverse regions, from West Africa's Sierra Leone to East Africa's South Africa, each exhibiting subtle variations in plumage and geographical distribution.

These adaptable birds thrive at altitudes ranging from 100 to 3,000 meters. Omnivorous by nature, the Lemon Dove subsists on a diet of fruits, seeds, mollusks, and insects. The female lays two creamy eggs in a nest, ensuring the continuation of this resilient species.

With a wide range and stable population trend, the Lemon Dove is classified as a species of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). This versatile bird, with its captivating plumage and diverse diet, continues to enchant observers in the African wilderness.

In the dense, verdant deciduous forests of eastern North America, once soared the majestic passenger pigeon (Ectopistes migratorius). Named for its nomadic tendencies, this now extinct species was a testament to nature's grandeur and mankind's devastating impact.

Morphologically diverse, males sported azure neck feathers against a gray upper body and lighter underparts, contrasting the browner, less iridescent females. The juveniles mimicked their female counterparts but lacked the male's distinctive iridescence.

This migratory avian, once numbering billions, inhabited the Great Lakes region primarily, yet its flocks were sighted across North America. Known for their communal roosting and breeding habits, passenger pigeons could reach speeds of 100 km/h, making them formidable flyers.

However, their existence was short-lived. Intensified hunting by Native Americans and Europeans, habitat loss due to deforestation, and the need for large breeding populations led to their rapid decline and eventual extinction. The last confirmed wild passenger pigeon fell in 1902, with the last known individual, Martha, perishing at the Cincinnati Zoo in 1914. Their demise serves as a stark reminder of anthropogenic extinction's consequences.

Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus grouped both passenger pigeons and mourning doves under Columba macroura in his seminal work, Systema Naturae, seemingly unaware of their distinct differences. A poignant reminder of a once thriving species, now forever lost to the annals of history.

In the vibrant landscapes of the Caribbean, the white-crowned pigeon (Patagioenas leucocephala) roams, a fruit and seed-eating avian species. Discovered and depicted by naturalists like John Ray, Hans Sloane, Mark Catesby, and Carl Linnaeus in the 18th century, this pigeon was initially classified as Columba leucocephala before being reclassified under Patagioenas in the 19th century.

Measuring between 29-35 cm long, with a wingspan of 48-59 cm, and weighing 150-301 g, this species is similar in size to the common rock pigeon but leaner, featuring a more square tail. Adults exhibit a range of shades from slate gray to almost black plumage, adorned with an iridescent collar-patch that gleams white and green under good light. The distinctive crown-patch varies in shade from a bright white in males to a more gray-white in females. Juveniles are initially greyish-brown, lacking the nape pattern and white iris, and showing only a few pale feathers on the crown.

Breeding predominantly in the Bahamas, Cuba, Jamaica, Antigua, and other Caribbean islands, this species also inhabits smaller numbers in Hispaniola, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, the Cayman Islands, Anguilla, and along the Caribbean coast of Central America. In the United States, they are found only in the Florida Keys, Everglades, and southern tip of mainland Florida. During winter months, these pigeons often migrate to the Caribbean islands, with occasional sightings as far south as Fuerte Island, Colombia. Their melodious call, a deep coo-cura-coo or coo-croo, resonates through their diverse habitats, a testament to their resilience and charm in the heart of the tropics.

In the vibrant tapestry of the Caribbean, a distinctive presence stands out - the scaly-naped pigeon (Patagioenas squamosa), also known as the red-necked pigeon. This large avian species, measuring 14-16 inches, dominates the skies with its slate grey body and maroon-hued nape, featuring scaly-textured plumage that mirrors its common name.

A resident of the Greater Antilles (except Jamaica and Bahamas) and Lesser Antilles, the scaly-naped pigeon thrives in rainforests but can also be found in drier lowland woodlands near human settlements. Unfortunately, their numbers have declined due to hunting pressure, especially in Puerto Rico, and hurricanes that ravage their habitats.

In their daily lives, scaly-naped pigeons forage alone or in small groups, feeding on the fruits and seeds of trees while occasionally associating with Rock doves, Eared doves, and Zenaida doves. They nest high up in trees, laying one to two eggs at a time. Their wary nature makes them quick to flee when danger approaches.

The scaly-naped pigeon's existence continues to be documented through ongoing studies such as those conducted by Rivera-Milán et al., providing valuable insights into their population dynamics and association patterns, even in the face of adversity.

In the verdant realms of tropical America, the scaled pigeon (Patagioenas speciosa) thrives. A large New World dove, it spans from southern Mexico to Western Ecuador, Brazil's south, Argentina's north, and Trinidad.

The species is a common inhabitant of semi-open forests, constructing stick nests in trees and laying two white eggs. Its flight is swift and direct, characterized by regular beats punctuated by the occasional sharp flick of its wings. Wary by nature, it frequents areas where hunting is less prevalent.

First documented in 1789 by Johann Friedrich Gmelin, the scaled pigeon was initially classified under Columba before being reclassified in the resurrected genus Patagioenas in 2001 due to a molecular phylogenetic study. Its name originates from Ancient Greek, meaning "to clatter"and "pigeon."

Adult males possess purple-brown plumage with a scaly neck and underparts, while females are dull brown. Both genuss have red eyes, legs, and bills with a white tip. These solitary birds primarily feed on forest fruits and seeds, supplementing their diet with fresh leaves and occasionally crops. Their call is a deep series of cooing cro ku-ks that distinguishes them from relatives such as the white-crowned pigeon.

Nesting sites vary, ranging from ground-level ferns like Pteridium aquilinum to tree fern canopies like Cyathea cyatheoides. The pearl kite poses a natural threat as a predator to the scaled pigeon's offspring.

In the verdant expanses of South America, the Picazuro Pigeon (Patagioenas picazuro) reigns. This towering avian, with wings that span up to 22 inches and a weight nearing a pound, bears a striking resemblance to the spotted dove, distinguished by azure plumage speckled with brown and white dashes on its neck.

Hailing from Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay, this common species stretches across 5.8 million square kilometers of diverse habitats - from woodlands and forests to cultivated lands. Despite a lack of precise population data, the Picazuro Pigeon's numbers appear to be on the rise.

The Picazuro Pigeon is an opportunistic feeder, foraging mainly on the ground for seeds and grains. Its reproductive cycle is year-round, with both parents building a fragile nest of sticks and laying a single egg that they incubate together. Upon hatching, the chick is nourished first by crop milk, and later regurgitated seeds from its parents' meals.

This resilient species thrives in Latin America's rich tapestry of life, adding another layer to the continent's diverse ecosystem.

In the vibrant landscapes of Colombia, Venezuela, and the Dutch Antilles, the enigmatic Bare-eyed Pigeon (Patagioenas corensis) thrives. This species, believed to share a common ancestry with the Picazuro Pigeon, Spot-winged Pigeon, and Scaled Pigeon, has captivated ornithologists for its distinctive features.

Male and female Bare-eyed Pigeons measure between 30-37 cm in length and weigh around 274 grams. Their unique plumage boasts a mauve-pink head, neck, and breast, contrasted by an orange-brown eye encircled by bare skin, a narrow blue ring, and a wider reddish-brown area. The back sports a bronze-black and pale brown scaled appearance, while the lower back and rump are pale bluish gray.

The Bare-eyed Pigeon can be found in arid landscapes of thorn scrub, cactus, and acacia, as well as mangroves and cultivated areas. It inhabits heights of up to 400 meters on the mainland but is typically much lower. Its introduction to Saint Martin Island in 2013 led to a swift population growth and expansion across the island.

A rhythmic series of coos form the Bare-eyed Pigeon's song, with calls including "rwhoh...woh-hu-whOAh...woh-hu-whOAh..woh-hu-whOAh...". These vocalizations may signify a breeding cycle triggered by rain. The species constructs thin twig nests in trees or shrubs, offering a striking visual spectacle.

The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) classifies the Bare-eyed Pigeon as Least Concern, despite being heavily hunted, due to its seeming population resilience. Known for its reticence, this captivating bird continues to intrigue and fascinate those who encounter it.

In the vast, arid landscapes of South America, the spot-winged pigeon (Patagioenas maculosa) thrives, its distinctive presence echoing through open woodland and scrub. This species, found across Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, Peru, and Uruguay, is a member of the Columbidae family, sharing close ties with the picazuro pigeon, bare-eyed pigeon, and scaled pigeon, according to some ornithologists.

The nominate subspecies, P. m. maculosa, stands 32 to 33 cm tall, its adult male adorned in a blend of dull purplish pink on the head and breast, contrasting with dark brown wings spotted with creamy white. The female is similar but more subdued, while the juvenile is a softer version of both.

In contrast, P. m. albipennis, found in Peru, Bolivia, Argentina, and Chile, boasts a bluish-gray plumage with purplish undertones, particularly prominent in males. Both subspecies exhibit unique adaptations to their respective habitats: P. m. maculosa inhabits lowlands up to 1000 meters, shunning towns, while P. m. albipennis favors higher elevations, 2000 to 4300 meters, and is locally common in urban areas.

Feeding predominantly on seeds, including rice and sunflower, P. m. maculosa also samples fruit and leaves. The diet of P. m. albipenniss remains undiscovered. Both subspecies are social creatures, foraging on the ground in flocks.

Breeding occurs year-round in Argentina, with P. m. maculosa constructing fragile nests in trees and laying one or two eggs. The breeding habits of P. m. albipenniss are as yet unstudied, though increased calling in October may signal the start of the breeding season. The spot-winged pigeon's rhythmic song continues to resonate through the continent, a testament to its resilient and evolving presence.

In the vast tapestry of American wildlife, the band-tailed pigeon (Patagioenas fasciata) stands out as one of its largest avian inhabitants. Reaching lengths of 33 to 40 cm and weighing between 226-268 grams, these birds exhibit a distinctive appearance: a predominantly gray plumage softened by a faint pink hue on the head and underparts, an almost white belly, and a tail marked with a pale band, earning them their English name.

Their wings are thick-based and pointed, spanning 66 cm. Males and females display minimal size differences, with males slightly larger but overlapping in weight. The bill and feet are yellow, offering clear identification at close range. The neck sports a green iridescence adjacent to the thin white collar on the nape, visible only in adult males. Juveniles bear a scaly appearance due to pale feather edges above, lacking both the white collar and neck iridescence.

The band-tailed pigeon is part of a superspecies that includes the Chilean pigeon (P. araucana) and ring-tailed pigeon (P. caribaea). Six subspecies exist, divided into two groups: the northern group inhabiting the Pacific Coast from Alaska to Baja California and the interior western U.S., and the southern group dwelling in Central America and the Andes of South America. The bill color distinguishes these groups; the northern group displays a black-tipped yellow bill, while the southern group sports an all-yellow bill.

The genus Patagioenas is genetically closest to the extinct passenger pigeon, making the band-tailed pigeon a prime candidate for potential reintroduction efforts aimed at resurrecting its extinct relative.

In the wilds of Chile and Argentina, the Chilean Pigeon (Patagioenas araucana) reigns, its monotypic domain stretching from Coquimbo south to Aysén, and venturing into Argentina's southern borderlands. This species shares a potential superspecies with the ring-tailed and band-tailed pigeons.

Standing 35-37 cm tall and weighing between 220-340 g, the adult male boasts vibrant reddish purple plumage, an iridescent green nape adorned with a narrow white stripe, gray lower back, rump, and tail, and a black-banded tail. The orange eye is encircled by a thin ring of pink or yellow, with bare purple skin beneath. Females exhibit a more subdued brown palette, while juveniles are generally grayscale.

Inhabiting southern temperate forests dominated by Araucaria and Nothofagus, this pigeon can also be found in drier forests in the north and foraging in open habitats adjacent to forests. Its primary diet consists of fruit gleaned from tree canopies, though seeds in open areas aren't overlooked.

Breeding occurs between December and March, possibly extending to May, with colonies of up to several hundred pairs nesting deep within the forest's interior, often in bamboo thickets. Nests are simple platforms of sticks, offering an unobstructed view of the single egg. The Chilean Pigeon's distinctive song resonates as a series of deep, rhythmic "hoos"or a resonant "deep doubled hooo-HOOOO hooo-HOOOO hooo-HOOOO..."

The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) classifies the Chilean Pigeon as Least Concern. Yet, deforestation of old-growth forests looms as a significant concern due to its impact on habitat fragmentation and reduction.

In the verdant highlands of Jamaica, the ring-tailed pigeon (Patagioenas caribaea) reigns, a species unique to this island paradise. This avian beauty, measuring 38 to 48.5 cm in length for males and 38 to 43 cm for females, boasts a vibrant plumage that tells a visual tale. The male's head, neck, and underparts are a rich red or pinkish hue, contrasting sharply with the metallic green or bronze patch on its hindneck and the brownish gray upperparts. A slate band graces the tail, darker near the body, and the eye is framed by orange and bordered by bare red skin. Females share similar features but with an olive or brown cast to their wings, a less metallic hindneck, and reddish underparts.

Nestled within the island's wet highland forests and limestone forests of the Cockpit Country, this elusive bird thrives at altitudes up to 2000 meters. Its diet consists solely of fruit, often plucked while perched upside-down in trees.

Breeding season commences in spring and summer, during which the ring-tailed pigeon constructs a nest—a thick platform fashioned from twigs, leaves, and bark—high within hidden foliage in a tree. The IUCN initially classified this species as Critically Endangered, but subsequent assessments label it Vulnerable due to ongoing hunting and forest destruction that pose significant threats to its diminishing population and range. This regal bird's haunting call, a series of four to five cooing notes—cooOOh...cooOOh...cooOOh—echoes through the Jamaican wilderness, a poignant reminder of the precarious balance between nature and human activity.

In the tropical Americas, the majestic pale-vented pigeon (Patagioenas cayennensis) roams. A member of the Patagioenas clade, this large, grey-hued bird exhibits a subtle coppery gloss on its nape, distinguishing it from other species in its family.

Resident breeders in southern Mexico to Bolivia and northern Argentina, Tobago, Trinidad, they are localized on the latter island. While vagrants stray into adjacent regions, they've never established a permanent presence beyond their native range.

Measuring 30–32 cm long, adult males boast a purple head, breast, and upperpart plumage with a whitish throat and dark grey lower back and tail. The lower underparts are pale grey, the bill black, legs red, and iris and eyering matching the beak. Females resemble males but are duller, immatures are greyish-brown, and the southern subspecies P. c. andersoni has white lower underparts.

The pale-vented pigeon's call is a soft kuk kuk croo-ooo, distinctively similar to its close relatives yet unique. In the field, it can resemble the scaled pigeon (P. speciosa), but their calls and appearances differentiate them.

This species thrives at forest edges, riverbanks, and partially open areas with trees, feeding mainly on small fruits, berries, and seeds. Solitary by nature, it forms small flocks at drinking areas. Its high, fast, direct flight is characteristic of pigeons, punctuated by occasional wing flicks. The pale-vented pigeon performs a breeding display with a semi-circular glide to its original perch, building a small twig nest in a tree and laying one white egg. Widespread and common, it is classified as a species of least concern by the IUCN.

In the sprawling landscape of coastal Mexico and Central America, the red-billed pigeon (Patagioenas flavirostris) stands out. This robust species, extending from southern Texas to Costa Rica, thrives in lowlands and coastal regions. Known for its dark, slate-gray plumage with a maroon head, neck, and wing coverts, it's distinguished by red eyes with an orange orbital ring, a red bill tipped yellow, and blue-gray belly and tail coverts.

With a length of 12–15 inches and weighing between 230–425 grams, the red-billed pigeon is mid to large in size among birds. Its call, a high-pitched coooo followed by three cuk-c'-c'-coos, echoes through dry forests. Upon taking flight, its wings create a distinctive clapping sound.

The red-billed pigeon boasts four subspecies: restricta, madrensis, flavirostris, and minima, inhabiting various habitats from dry woods to riverside wetlands. Though common in summer along the Rio Grande, they are uncommon during winter across their range. With a call that's as unique as its habitat, the red-billed pigeon is a captivating testament to nature's diversity.

In the heart of South America, a cryptic beauty unfurls its wings—the Maranon Pigeon (Patagioenas oenops). Known variously as the Peruvian pigeon or Salvin's pigeon, this enigmatic bird graces Ecuador and Peru.

Male Maranon Pigeons stand tall at 34 cm, with females measuring 31 to 34 cm. They weigh around 276g, their plumage a tapestry of colors—adult males displaying a reddish head, neck, and breast that gradate into blue-gray upperparts with purplish undertones on the mid-back and wings. Underparts are light gray, while the tail remains dark gray. The eye is unique, encircled by an intricate pattern of black, orange, and blue rings, with bare blue-gray skin around it. Females and juveniles exhibit a subdued, brownish palette.

Within its range of 850 to 2400 meters in elevation, the Maranon Pigeon migrates between riparian forests and dryer upslope woodlands. Its diet primarily consists of ripe coca seeds and fruits from Cordia lutea trees. However, much remains unknown about its breeding patterns and songs, which are characterized by a rhythmic series of coos—an enchanting symphony that begins with a single note, followed by triple notes repeated: 'rwhoOoh...pUh-hu-whoOOo...pUh-hu-whoOOo...'

The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) lists the Maranon Pigeon as near threatened, citing habitat loss and degradation as the primary culprits behind its declining range and population.

In the heart of the Greater Antilles, the plain pigeon (Patagioenas inornata) reigns. This columbid species calls Cuba, Hispaniola, Jamaica, and Puerto Rico home, thriving within forested landscapes, coastal scrub, and swamps.

At first glance, the plain pigeon appears a large-bodied avian, its plumage an azure-gray hue, contrasted by a red-wine wash on its head, hindneck, breast, and wings. In flight, a striking wing bar emerges, revealing a white leading edge. The species boasts a pale yellow iris, dark red legs and feet, and a black bill. Females are slightly smaller and less vibrant than males, while juveniles bear a browner plumage with paler wings and dark eyes.

The plain pigeon shares close ties with the red-billed pigeon (P. flavirostria) and the Maranon pigeon (P. oenops), both found in Central and South America, though it stands larger than its counterparts at 38 cm long. Historically, three subspecies of the plain pigeon were recognized - P. i. inornata from Cuba and Hispaniola, P. i. exigua from Jamaica, and P. i. wetmorei from Puerto Rico - though today, it is treated as a single species.

During the 1970s, the Puerto Rican population was on the brink of extinction. Ornithologist Richard C. Banks proposed that the current population may have been naturally or human-assistedly reintroduced from Hispaniola. A conservation program has since been established to protect this species, which now numbers a few thousand individuals.

In the dense tropical rainforests of South America, the elusive Plumbeous Pigeon (Patagioenas plumbea) resides - from Colombia's eastern Andes to Brazil's Amazon basin. This species, closely related to the Ruddy and Short-billed pigeons, encompasses six distinct subspecies, each subtly varying in the iridescence of their plumage.

Males exhibit a dark gray head and underparts with a pinkish or purplish tinge, contrasting with a drab olive back and wings speckled with bronzy spots. Females display a less intense purple wash and more pronounced neck spots. The juveniles are duller, with rusty-edged feathers that set them apart from the adults.

The Plumbeous Pigeon's range extends from Colombia's west slope to the Guianas and eastern Amazonia in Brazil. Each subspecies inhabits specific geographical regions, such as the Andes, the Rio Negro, or the Rio Tapajós. Foraging high within the canopy, these pigeons subsist on fruit, seeds, and mistletoe drupes in Brazil.

Despite their captivating appearance, little is known about the Plumbeous Pigeon's breeding phenology or migratory patterns. Their distinct vocalizations - a repeated rhythmic phrase of 2-5 coos or a drawn-out, overslurred 'rrrrrow' - suggest that these subtly differentiated species may be more varied than initially thought.

In the verdant canopies of Central and South America, a distinctive avian species reigns—the Ruddy Pigeon (Patagioenas subvinacea). This largish pigeon, measuring 28 cm long and weighing 170 g, showcases an unpatterned wine-purple plumage that transitions to rufous on the back. The tail and primary flight feathers are dark brown, bill black, legs purple-red, and iris red or dull yellow in certain regions.

The Ruddy Pigeon's call is loud and high-pitched, featuring a unique coo sound with notable pauses between notes—much like its kin. It shares habitat with the short-billed pigeon (P. nigrirostris) in Central America but can be differentiated by its faster, less complex call.

This species resides at altitudes ranging from 1500 meters to the timberline, foraging for fruits and berries, particularly mistletoe fruit. They are often seen in pairs nesting high in small trees with a rudimentary twig platform and laying a single white egg. The Ruddy Pigeon's wide range, though threatened by deforestation, classifies it as Least Concern by the IUCN.

In the dense forest canopies of southern Mexico to northwestern Colombia, a regal avian rules - the short-billed pigeon (Patagioenas nigrirostris). Part of the subgenus Oenoenas, this species shares distinct calls with smaller, plainer relatives.

Measuring 26.5 cm long and weighing approximately 150 g, the short-billed pigeon sports an unpatterned wine-purple plumage, transitioning to browner hues on the belly and olive-brown on the back. The tail, primary flight feathers, bill, legs, and eyes are blackish, purple-red, or greyish-brown in juveniles.

The short-billed pigeon's complex, high-pitched call - coo... cu-COO k’CO coohoo - is often heard singly or with pauses between calls. Typically seen in pairs foraging for mistletoe, fruits, berries, grit, and small invertebrates, they are also spotted on tracks and roadsides.

In southern Central America, this species yields to the ruddy pigeon (Patagioenas subvinacea) at higher altitudes. The short-billed pigeon's song is slower and more complex compared to its close relative.

In the verdant expanse of Colombia and Ecuador's Chocó Biogeographic Region, a cryptic avian resides—the dusky pigeon (Patagioenas goodsoni). A species that shares kinship with the ruddy, short-billed, and plumbeous pigeons, it exhibits a mesmerizing palette of colors.

Adorned in shades of gray, purplish gray, olive brown, and bronzy gray, this 24 cm avian boasts a captivating plumage. Its head, throat, and breast are either gray or purplish gray, with a gloss of purple on the nape. The back, rump, and wings are an array of olive brown or dark purplish brown, bronzy gray wings that sometimes shimmer with a hint of purple.

The dusky pigeon's song is a series of three high-pitched coos—"whoah..pup..pup"—while its call echoes as a purring, drawn-out "rrrow."Despite its melodious serenade, the species' life history remains largely elusive. Its foraging behavior and diet remain undocumented, as does the breeding phenology beyond noting captures between January and May, and August.

Though classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, this enigmatic creature faces an uncertain future. While it thrives locally within its restricted range, the looming threat of deforestation looms large. The dusky pigeon's captivating presence and tantalizing mysteries make it a species worth protecting.

In the realm of avian diversity, few species captivate like the Malagasy turtle dove (Nesoenas picturatus). This bird, a member of the Columbidae family, boasts several subspecies, one being the extinct Rodrigues pigeon (N. rodericanus), once suspected to be a subspecies of N. picturatus.

The pink pigeon (N. mayeri) shares close genetic ties with the Malagasy turtle dove, forming a distinct lineage separate from typical pigeons and turtle doves. This unique taxonomy places them either within Streptopelia or, more accurately, in their own genus Nesoenas.

With habitats spanning Madagascar, Mauritius, Comoros, Réunion, Mayotte, Seychelles, and the Chagos archipelago, the Malagasy turtle dove is a species of least concern according to the IUCN, despite some island populations teetering on the brink of rarity.

In the remote Mascarene island of Rodrigues, a spectral avian ruled—the extinct Rodrigues pigeon (Nesoenas rodericanus). Known from subfossil remains and early accounts by explorers Leguat and Tafforet, this bird resembled a miniature imperial pigeon or Gallicolumba ground dove.

Famed naturalist Milne-Edwards, in his 1873 study, identified the Rodrigues pigeon as possibly the closest relative of the Raphidae, sharing roots with the dodo and the Rodrigues solitaire. Despite this kinship, it bore no close relation to didine pigeons.

Tame and confiding, these slate-gray birds would gather around settlers' tables for melon seeds. Sadly, by 1726—the last account by Tafforet—rats had infested their nesting sites, marking the end of this spectral species in the mid-18th century.

In the enchanting island sanctuary of Mauritius, the rare Pink Pigeon (Nesoenas mayeri) reigns. Once teetering on the brink of extinction, with mere ten individuals remaining in 1991, this resilient avian has since risen from near oblivion due to conservation efforts spearheaded by the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust since 1977.

Classified within a monotypic genus, the Pink Pigeon is genetically linked to its Malagasy cousin, the turtle dove (Nesoenas picturata). Its unique evolutionary lineage has earned it a distinct classification apart from Streptopelia or Columba.

An adult Pink Pigeon stands approximately 36-38 cm tall and weighs about 350 grams. Distinctive features include pinkish-grey plumage, dark brown wings, a rust-colored tail, and dark brown eyes ringed in red skin. Their voice echoes with a short, hardened "hoo hoo"flight call and a cooing territorial call from the male.

The species is endemic to Mauritius and its neighboring Île aux Aigrettes, their preferred habitat being native upland evergreen forests or coastal forests devoid of invasive species like Chinese Guava (Psidium cattleianum) or privet (Ligustrum robustum). The Pink Pigeon, a non-migratory creature, thrives within five monitored habitats within Black River Gorges National Park and Île aux Aigrettes as of 2016. Conservation efforts continue to ensure this captivating species remains a vital part of Mauritius's rich natural heritage.

In the realm of the avian kingdom, the European Turtle Dove (Streptopelia turtur) stands as a threatened jewel, belonging to the Columbidae family. A migratory species, it breeds across the southwestern Palearctic, from North Africa to western Siberia, and journeys to sub-Saharan Africa for winter.

First formally described by Carl Linnaeus in 1758, this species was initially categorized as Columba turtur before being reclassified under Streptopelia in 1855. The specific epithet 'turtur' is a Latin term imitating the bird's melodic song. Today, four subspecies are recognized, each distinctively inhabiting different regions from Europe to Iran and western China.

The European Turtle Dove distinguishes itself with its slender frame, measuring 26-28 cm in length, boasting a wingspan of 47-53 cm, and weighing between 100-156 g. Its neck sports a black-and-white striped patch, and its tail is unique—wedge-shaped with a dark centre and white borders and tips. When viewed from below, this pattern transforms into a chevron against a white background, visible when the bird stoops to drink or raises its spread tail.

The mature European Turtle Dove is characterized by a blue-grey head, neck, flanks, rump, cinnamon-coloured wings mottled with black, a vinaceous breast, and white abdomen and undertail coverts. Its bill is distinctively hued. A few other doves share the 'turtle' name within their genus Streptopelia or Nesoenas, found across Asia, Africa, and Madagascar. Despite its name, the European Turtle Dove's moniker bears no connection to the reptile of the same name.

In the vast expanse of East Africa and South Arabia, a captivating avian resides - the dusky turtle dove (Streptopelia lugens). This enigmatic bird, measuring roughly 28-30 centimeters long, boasts a distinctive slate gray plumage, adorned with an intricate pattern of black neck patches and rufous-edged wing coverts.

Its visage is defined by a slightly paler gray face, vibrant orange or yellow eyes encircled in purple, and a purplish slate-gray beak. A closer inspection reveals its legs, painted red, adding to the bird's mesmerizing allure. Juveniles exhibit a more subdued palette compared to their adult counterparts.

The dusky turtle dove thrives in the lush Afrotropical Highlands Biome, preferring the sanctuary of montane forests, forest edges, and woodland gardens at elevations between 1,800 and 3,200 meters. This elusive bird subsists on a diet of seeds, insects, and mollusks, foraged from the ground. It is an active diurnal creature, often observed alone or in small groups of up to ten.

In terms of social structure, the dusky turtle dove forms monogamous pairings. Females construct nests, intricate twig platforms lined with rootlets, usually nestled within tree canopies at heights of 2-3 meters. Each clutch typically consists of two eggs, upon which both parents share incubation duties for approximately twenty days and nestling care for around fourteen to sixteen days thereafter.

In the heart of Cameroon, Nigeria, and southwestern Chad, amidst Gambia, Senegal, Mali, and potentially Togo, dwells the elusive Adamawa turtle dove (Streptopelia hypopyrrha). Often mistaken for its dusky counterpart, this species boasts a distinct black shoulder patch, silver-grey face, blue-grey head, and a unique pink belly and breast.

Measuring 30 to 31 centimeters in length and weighing between 147 to 187 grams, the Adamawa turtle dove inhabits various habitats, from sea level to 1200 meters. It thrives at forest edges, wooded ravines, gallery forests, suburban gardens, and farmland.

The species' behavior remains largely undiscovered, yet it feeds on seeds in flocks of up to 60 birds during the non-breeding season and sometimes associates with other dove species. Breeding occurs at the end of the dry season, with both parents building a flimsy nest and incubating two white eggs.

Despite their enigmatic nature, the Adamawa turtle dove is deemed a species of least concern by BirdLife International, suggesting that these captivating creatures continue to thrive in their diverse habitats.

In the realm of avian diversity, the Oriental turtle dove (Streptopelia orientalis) stands prominently among its kin. Native to Central Asia and Japan, this species exhibits geographical variation in plumage, resulting in six distinct subspecies.

Named for its collar-like appearance—streptos peleia, in Ancient Greek—the Oriental turtle dove's range spans far eastward from Siberian taiga to tropical India. Its diurnal habits find it predominantly foraging on the ground for grains.

Subspecies like the western Oriental turtle dove (S. o. meena) migrate south in winter, while eastern populations remain sedentary. Distinctive tail patterns aid identification during migration, with orientalis exhibiting a grey tip and meena featuring a white one. The nominate form is characterized by grey on the forehead and crown, unlike the reddish-brown head of S. o. erythrocephala in southern India.

The Oriental turtle dove's appearance mirrors that of the Turtle dove, but its size—particularly among the subspecies orientalis—approaches that of a collared dove. The species' unique distribution and diverse subspecies make it an intriguing subject for study and observation.

In the heart of Indonesia's tropical islands, the Sunda Collared Dove (Streptopelia bitorquata) resides, a species distinct yet often mistaken for the Eurasian Collared Dove.

Native to Java, Bali, and various other islands, this medium-sized bird boasts a unique blend of colors—azure neck and chest, caramel underside, grey wings, and 'eggshell' tail feathers beneath. A black collar, half-concealed by a white highlight, sets it apart from its Eurasian cousin.

Feeding habits mirror those of other collared doves, with a preference for cereal grains, seeds, and occasional berries. In times of scarcity, they may turn to caterpillars or aphids. The Sunda Collared Dove is closely related to the Philippine Collared Dove but differs significantly in distribution and adaptability.

Unlike the Eurasian species that thrives in urban environments worldwide, the Sunda Collared Dove remains confined to Indonesia's islands, largely due to its slower rate of global dispersal. First noted in 1809, it continues to elude extensive documentation, existing as an enigma among the avian world's more visible inhabitants.

In the vibrant archipelago of the Philippines, nestles the enigmatic Philippine Collared Dove (Streptopelia dusumieri). Known locally as 'bato-bato de collar,' this dove species boasts a unique identity, distinct from its Sunda counterpart.

The dove presents an intriguing blend of browns and grays, adorned with a short collar of large dark scales, a pinkish neck flush, and gray head—an ode to the urban landscapes it favors, both in its native Philippines and introduced habitats of Guam and Northern Mariana Islands.

Once abundant, this species faces a decline due to fierce competition from Spotted Doves and Red-collared Doves within its native range. In Guam, where it remains relatively common, the nest success rate is alarmingly low—only 2 to 5% in urban areas and 0 to 3% in forest habitats.

Conservation efforts are underway to assess the impact of competing species, identify strongholds, and monitor population trends. The future for this captivating dove remains precarious, underscoring the urgent need for its protection.

locations: native from Europe to China; widely introduced elsewhere

data.summary:
In the heart of Europe, Asia, and northern Africa, the Eurasian collared dove (Streptopelia decaocto) flourishes, extending its reach to Japan, North America, and Caribbean islands through human introduction.

First described by Hungarian naturalist Imre Frivaldszky in 1838 as a variety of the domesticated barbary dove, this species now resides in the genus Streptopelia, named for its distinctive "collar"of feathers. The Burmese collared dove was once considered a subspecies but is now recognized as a distinct species.

The Eurasian collared dove shares a superspecies with the Sunda collared dove of southeast Asia and the African collared dove, its cousin from Sub-Saharan Africa. While difficult to distinguish visually among silent birds, their calls are uniquely different: the African species emitting a soft, purring "Cou'crrrrroouw,"in stark contrast to the Eurasian's three-note cooing.

The Eurasian collared dove, with an average length of 32 cm, weight of 125 grams, and wingspan of 47 cm, is a medium-sized dove that stands out for its grey-buff to pinkish-grey plumage, blue-grey underwing patch, and long tail feathers. The genus's name harks back to ancient Greek mythology, where the call of the collared dove was said to tell the tale of an overworked maid who begged Zeus for justice.

In the realm of avian diversity, the Burmese collared dove (Streptopelia xanthocycla) stands out. A close relative to the Eurasian collared dove, it distinguishes itself with a vibrant yellow eye ring and darker plumage. Reaching lengths of 30-32 cm, it shares size but not song—its melody is a unique two-note coo, contrasting the Eurasian's three-note serenade.

Originating in the heart of Myanmar's Irrawaddy River basin, this species once held a territorial identity. However, its melodious call has been heard, though sparsely, in adjacent China. Yet, firm evidence remains elusive, leaving its distribution somewhat enigmatic. The Burmese collared dove—a testament to the intricate symphony of the world's avian domain.

In the vast Sahel and Horn of Africa landscapes, the African Collared Dove (Streptopelia roseogrisea) thrives, its habitat ranging from arid lands to water sources. This species, distinguished by its unique coloration and potential interbreeding with domestic doves, exhibits a captivating blend of greyish brown and pinkish hues, with flight feathers nearly black and wing edges tinged blue.

Two subspecies are recognized: S. roseogrisea roseogrisea, inhabiting the Sahel region south of the Sahara Desert; and S. roseogrisea arabica, found in northeast Sudan to Eritrea and Somalia, as well as southwestern Arabia. These doves forage on grass seeds, berries, insects, and snails, their diet a testament to their adaptability in harsh environments.
